summary:
During muscle contraction, myosin heads release from actin when ATP breaks down into ADP and a phosphate group, facilitating relaxation between contractions.
Myosin head detachment occurs upon ATP cleavage leading to conformational changes allowing it to dissociate from actin for rest periods. To summarize:

During muscle contraction cycles, myosin detaches from actin due to ATP breaking down; afterward, there’s an opportunity for reattachment until next stimulation triggers another round of force generation through repeated steps including power stroke followed by recovery phase enabling continuous movement or sustained tension as needed physiologically. 

Key Points Condensed:
- Detachment happens post-hydrolysis
- Allows subsequent attachment/reaction loop

This captures essential aspects without extraneous details while addressing core mechanisms involved directly with contractile function regulated via energy metabolism pathways within sarcomeres comprising striated tissue structures like those found primarily innervated under somatic nervous system control governing voluntary movements among other roles across different types of muscular systems throughout body physiology functioning harmoniously together maintaining homeostasis amidst varying demands placed upon them dynamically over time according context specific conditions encountered daily living activities ranging widely depending individual needs health status etc...
